request.question: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?

title: Guan (bird)
Spotted elachura
Springwood State High School
Wakaleo vanderleuri
Koala
Rhagophthalmidae
Palorchestes
Centenary Heights State High School
Didunculus
Itatiella
sentences: The guans are a number of bird genera which make up the largest group in the family Cracidae.
They are found mainly in northern South America, southern Central America, and a few adjacent Caribbean islands.
There is also the peculiar horned guan ("Oreophasis derbianus") which is not a true guan, but a very distinct and ancient cracid with no close living relatives (Pereira "et al." 2002).
The spotted elachura or spotted wren-babbler ("Elachura formosa") is a species of passerine bird found in the forests of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

In the past it was included in the babbler genus "Spelaeornis" as "S. formosus", but molecular phylogenetic studies in 2014 provided evidence that it was distinct from the babblers and part of a basal lineage (one that diverged early) with no other close living relatives within the passerine bird clade Passerida.
This led to the creation of a new family, Elachuridae, to accommodate just one species (a monotypic taxon).
Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.
The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.
Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.
In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).
Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.
This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.
It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.
The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.
The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.
The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.
It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.
The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.
It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.
The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .
Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.
Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.
These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.
The Rhagophthalmidae are a family of beetles within the larger Elateroidea group that include click-beetles.

Members of this beetle family have bioluminescent organs on the larvae and are closely related to the Phengodidae (glowworm beetles) and Lampyridae (fireflies), and were often included in one of these families as a subfamily—Rhagophthalminae.
Towards the end of the 20th century, they were increasingly seen as a distinct family.
But they might be the only reasonably close living relatives of the mysterious fireflies of genus "Pterotus", and could eventually end up in a Lampyridae subfamily with these.
They were mainly included in the Phengodidae for having similar adaptation for displaying glowing lights.
However, this seems to be due to convergent evolution.

If they indeed belong in the Lampyridae, they are related to a group of families that have either a distinct type of flashing lights (Luciolinae) or rely exclusively or nearly so on pheromones for communication (the others).
Palorchestes ('ancient leaper or dancer') is an extinct genus of terrestrial herbivorous marsupial of the family Palorchestidae.
The genus was endemic to Australia, living from the Late Miocene subepoch through the Pleistocene epoch (around 11.6 mya – 11,000 years ago), and thought to be in existence for approximately .

Centenary Heights State High School is a State High School located in the suburb of Centenary Heights in Toowoomba, Queensland, with an enrolment of around 1500 students (2015) and was one of Queensland's leading high schools with 11 OP's 1 (Overall Position) scored in 2012.
The school is one of several state high schools in Toowoomba, including Toowoomba State High School , Harristown State High School and Wilsonton State High School.
The tooth-billed pigeons are the only genus ("Didunculus") of the subfamily Didunculinae, in the pigeon and dove family, (Columbidae).
It has no close living relatives, but it has been shown to be genetically close to the dodo, and the genus name "Didunculus" means "little dodo".

The jaw and tongue structure, and the superficially parrotlike bill have suggested a relationship to the parrots, but these features have arisen from its specialised diet rather than any real relationship.
Itatiella ulei is a species of moss in the family Polytrichaceae.
It is the only species in the genus Itatiella.
The Polytrichaceae is a common family of mosses that does not have close living relatives.
Its small size and the inflexed leaf apex characterize "Itatiella ulei".
When this species grows directly exposed to sun at high elevations, it presents a similar aspect but can be distinguished based on the distal lamella cells which are single and rhombic.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?
hop1_docs: Wakaleo | Wakaleo (indigenous Australian "waka", "little", "small", and Latin "leo", "lion") was a genus of medium-sized thylacoleonids that lived in Australia in the early to late Miocene.  It was approximately 2.5 ft (80 cm) long, or the size of a dog.  Although much smaller than its close relative, the marsupial lion "(Thylacoleo carnifex)", "Wakaleo" would have been a successful hunter in its time.  It had teeth specially designed for cutting and stabbing.  The ocelot-sized predator "Wakaleo", along with its jaguar-sized relative "Thylacoleo", were actually related to the herbivore wombats.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Wakaleo vanderleuri | Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).  Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.  This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.  It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.  The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.  The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.

Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Muramura | "Muramura williamsi" is an extinct marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a dog, it was a herbivore.
Ngapakaldia | Ngapakaldia is an extinct genus of marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a sheep, it was a ground-dwelling herbivore.

Phalangeridae | The Phalangeridae are a family of nocturnal marsupials native to Australia, New Guinea and Eastern Indonesia, including the cuscuses, brushtail possums, and their close relatives.  Considered a type of possum, most species are arboreal, and they inhabit a wide range of forest habitats from alpine woodland to eucalypt forest and tropical jungle.
response.summary_1:
Koala (Phascolarctos cinereus) presence at Springwood State High School

- From passage [2]: Koala — described as an "arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia"; scientific name "Phascolarctos cinereus"; "its closest living relatives are the wombats."  
- From passage [4]: Springwood State High School — "is the home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds."  
- Ambiguity/missing facts: none for identifying the species in question; the passages unambiguously link the koala to both the biological description and the school's fauna. (No additional identification needed.)
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
  1. No additional retrieval needed — evidence present in passages [2] and [4].  
  2. (Optional verification) Fetch "Koala" article — inspect lede/infobox for scientific name and relation to wombats.  
  3. (Optional verification) Fetch "Springwood State High School" page — inspect body section mentioning on-campus fauna or "Facilities"/"Fauna" section.
hop2_docs: Muramura | "Muramura williamsi" is an extinct marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a dog, it was a herbivore.
Ngapakaldia | Ngapakaldia is an extinct genus of marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a sheep, it was a ground-dwelling herbivore.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Phascolarctidae | The Phascolarctidae ("Phasco" - pouch or bag, "larct"- from the Greek "arctos" meaning bear) are a family of marsupials of the order Diprotodontia, consisting of only one extant species, the koala, and six well-known fossil species, with another five less well known fossil species, and two fossil species of the genus "Koobor", whose taxonomy is debatable but are placed in this group.  The closest relatives of the Phascolarctidae are the wombats, which comprise the family Vombatidae.

Vombatiformes | The Vombatiformes are one of the three suborders of the large marsupial order Diprotodontia.  Five of the seven known families within this suborder are extinct; only the families Phascolarctidae, with the koala, and Vombatidae, with three extant species of wombat, survive.

Australian Koala Foundation | The Australian Koala Foundation (AKF) is an international not-for-profit scientific organisation that aims to diminish the threats to the survival of koalas and in doing so raise the awareness of the global community to help save endangered fauna and flora.  It is the principal non-profit, non-governmental organisation dedicated to the conservation and effective management of the wild koala and its habitat.

Marsupial | Marsupials are any members of the mammalian infraclass Marsupialia.  All extant marsupials are endemic to Australasia and the Americas.  A distinctive characteristic common to these species is that most of the young are carried in a pouch.  Well-known marsupials include kangaroos, wallabies, koalas, possums, opossums, wombats, and Tasmanian devils.
response.summary_2: Koala (Phascolarctos cinereus) presence at Springwood State High School. Koala is an arboreal, herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose closest living relatives are the wombats.
